coredata.dc:title: Multi format compatible visual and fluorometric detection of SEB toxin in nanogram range by carbon dot-DNA and acriflavine nano-assembly
coredata.dc:creator: Kumari, Sonam
Tiwari, Mayank
Das, Prolay
coredata.dc:description: A simple, sensitive biosensing platform for visual and fluorometric detection of Staphylococcal enterotoxin B (SEB) was developed using SEB-binding aptamer as recognition element and Carbon Dot (CD) and Acriflavine (Acf) as fluorescent probes. The assay is based on FRET between CD and Acf, the latter being intercalated in DNA duplex. SEB aptamer and partially complementary ssDNA constitute the duplex that denature in presence of SEB due to selective binding of the aptamer with SEB. SEB binding to aptamer lead to release of Acf in solution and disrupt FRET between CD and Acf. Subsequently, fluorescence emission due to Acf disappeared with emergence of CD fluorescence upon UV irradiation. A linear spectrophotometric response in the range of 0.5 ng/mL to 10 ng/mL of SEB concentration was obtained within 15 min of incubation. Interestingly, FRET inhibition and recovery of blue emission of CD is visually detectable in multiple format like test tube, 96 well-plate and microfluidic channels when irradiated with a hand held UV lamp only and without any machine intervention at 5 ng/mL sensitivity level. UV irradiated microfluidic channel is able to display visible changes from green to blue emission with as low as 0.01 ng of the toxin in 20 μL sample volume.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Staphylococcal enterotoxin B
Aptamer
Acriflavin
FRET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conjugation of CDs with SEB aptamer
Development of PDMS based microfluidic kit
Covalent conjugation of CD with aptamer
Spectroscopic investigation of interaction of CD-DNA system with Acf and SEB
Optimization of working condition of biosensing nanoassembly
Analytical performance and sensitivity of the biosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 1.5% Agarose gel electrophoresis showing differences in mobility of CD after conjugation with S1 and interaction with SEB.

A. Circular Dichroism spectra of CD-S1-S2 in presence and absence of Acf and SEB toxin. B. UV–vis spectra of CD,CD-S1-S2 conjugate and Acf. C. Steady state spectra of CD,CD-S1-S2 conjugate, Acf and probe solution in absence and presence of SEB. D.Time-resolved fluorescence decay of CD-S1-S2in presence and absence of Acf and SEB.In all the experiments, conc. of S1-S2 (in CD-S1-S2):0.1 nmol, Acf:0.3 nmol and SEB 5 ng/ml.

A.Variation of fluorescence recovery of CD of CD-S1-S2 (with Acf) after addition of SEB with pH. B. Demonstration of working temperature range of SEB detection through estimation of fluorescence recovery of CD-S1-S2 (with Acf).In all the experiments, conc. of S1-S2 (in CD-S1-S2): 0.1 nmol, Acf: 0.3 nmol and SEB 5 ng/ml.Plot represents average of three trials carried out under identical conditions.

A. Fluorescence spectra of biosensor system CD-S1-S2-Acf containing different concentration of SEB toxin B.Peak fluorescence change (F/F0-1) as a function of SEB toxin concentration. Inset: Peak fluorescence change (F/F0 -1) is linear with SEB concentration over the range of 0.1–10 ng/ml.Conc. of S1-S2 (in CD-S1-S2): 0.1 nmol, Acf: 0.3 nmol. Point in the plot was mean of three trials carried out under identical conditions.

Visual illustration of fluorescence transition from green to blue with different concentration of SEB. A. Test tube format B. 96 plate well format C. Microfluidic channel format, volume injected per lane of microfluidic channels is 20 μL. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Scheme for SEB detection based on aptamers-target affinity and FRET between CD and Acf.

head.ce:title: Multi format compatible visual and fluorometric detection of SEB toxin in nanogram range by carbon dot-DNA and acriflavine nano-assembly

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, IIT Patna, Bihta, Patna, 801106, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, sensitive biosensing platform for visual and fluorometric detection of Staphylococcal enterotoxin B (SEB) was developed using SEB-binding aptamer as recognition element and Carbon Dot (CD) and Acriflavine (Acf) as fluorescent probes. The assay is based on FRET between CD and Acf, the latter being intercalated in DNA duplex. SEB aptamer and partially complementary ssDNA constitute the duplex that denature in presence of SEB due to selective binding of the aptamer with SEB. SEB binding to aptamer lead to release of Acf in solution and disrupt FRET between CD and Acf. Subsequently, fluorescence emission due to Acf disappeared with emergence of CD fluorescence upon UV irradiation. A linear spectrophotometric response in the range of 0.5 ng/mL to 10 ng/mL of SEB concentration was obtained within 15 min of incubation. Interestingly, FRET inhibition and recovery of blue emission of CD is visually detectable in multiple format like test tube, 96 well-plate and microfluidic channels when irradiated with a hand held UV lamp only and without any machine intervention at 5 ng/mL sensitivity level. UV irradiated microfluidic channel is able to display visible changes from green to blue emission with as low as 0.01 ng of the toxin in 20 μL sample volume.

ce:section.ce:para: Staphylococcal enterotoxin B (SEB) is a protein exotoxin produced by gram-positive bacteria Staphylococcus aureus [1,2]. Apart from being involved in food poisoning that leads to severe diarrhea, nausea and intestinal cramping, the heat stable toxin is a super antigen that stimulates immune system leading to Gastroenteritis, making it a potential biological weapon threat [3–7]. Previously reported detection of SEB involve immunological principles coupled with colorimetric, electrochemical, fluorescence, chemiluminescence, piezoelectric, Surface-Enhanced Raman Scattering (SERS), surface plasmon resonance and spectrometry based strategies that clearly point towards the significance and challenges to detect this food toxin (Table S1, SI) [8–19]. Commercially available kits use antibody based reversed passive latex agglutination (RPLA) method for detection of SEB toxin along with other SE toxins in semi quantitative multistep assays with a detection limit of 0.5 ng/ml [20,21]. However, use of expensive antibodies, sophisticated instruments, long processing time, multiple labor intensive handling steps, poor detection sensitivity and high cost has kept the challenge wide open for developing sensitive yet inexpensive point-of-care detection solutions for SEB relevant to several countries and a variety of weather conditions. Herein, for the first time we demonstrate sensitive detection of nanoscale amount of SEB through a multi-format compatible and visually detectable aptasensing platform based on fluorescent Carbon Dot (CD).

DNA aptamers are being increasingly used in biosensing as attractive alternatives to antibodies as a biorecognition element due to their increased specificity, sensitivity, higher purity and lower cost of production [22–26]. Reportedly, a colorimetric aptasensor for SEB detection has been developed that show a visible transition from red to violet color upon addition of the toxin that initiate aggregation of gold nanoparticle after binding with its aptamer operating at ∼27 °C only [27]. Nevertheless, gold nanoparticles tend to aggregate spontaneously and at an accelerated rate at elevated temperatures that may limit longevity of the probe and its realistic use in universal weather conditions [28,29].

CD, the newest member of carbon nanofamily is being increasingly used in biosensing and bioimaging [30,31]. This is mainly due to the prudent use of excellent fluorescence properties of CD that also offer remarkable chemical and photochemical stability, biocompatibility, high quantum yield, water solubility along with ease and cost-effective method of synthesis. CD has been used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of fungal toxin Ochratoxin A, Aflatoxin B1 (OTA) and Tartrazine [32–34]. Although, aptamers-CD conjugates have noteworthy biosensing potential, but such combinations are under-researched in food chemistry related to toxin detection [35–37].

Herein, a novel, rapid and sensitive fluorescence based sensing platform for detection of SEB is reported where SEB aptamer is covalently conjugated to blue fluorescence emitting CD. A partially complementary ssDNA strand is hybridized to the SEB aptamer and the resulting dsDNA harbor calculated amount of green emitting Acriflavine (Acf) dye through intercalation [38]. CD and Acf form a FRET (Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer) pair where the partial DNA duplex holds them in close proximity. In the presence of the SEB, the aptamer sequence preferably binds with the toxin by breaking the hybridized DNA duplex union. This releases the intercalated Acf in solution, whereby the FRET is disrupted, leading to restoration of fluorescence of the CD. The transition is visually detectable under an UV light source in test tube, 96 well plate and microfluidic channel format where addition of toxin restores the blue fluorescence of the solution from green in a wide range of temperature. This strategy works equally well with toxin containing aqueous solution as well as food samples like milk and lentil soup samples. This is the first report that demonstrates nanomolar visual detection of SEB involving CD and aptamers that hint towards the potential and rational development of a point-of-care biosensing system for the detection of SEB in food samples.

Citric acid, branched polyethylenimine (BPEI), Acf and HPLC purified 5′-phosphorylated single stranded DNA (ssDNA, SEB aptamers, S1), partially complementary strand (S2), SEB and SEA toxins and sylgard 184 were purchased from Sigma. Methyl imidazole and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC.HCl) were obtained from Alfa Aesar. All other chemicals required for buffer preparation and gel electrophoresis were obtained from either Sigma or Merck and used without further purification. Nanopure water from Millipore was used in all experiments including spectroscopic studies.

UV-Visible absorption spectra were recorded on an UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Steady-state fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Japan). Time resolved fluorescence decays were measured on a time-resolved fluorescence spectrophotometer from Edinburgh Instruments (LifeSpec-II, UK) working on time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique.Circular Dichroism (CD)spectra were obtained on a Jasco J-1500 spectropolarimeter. All measurements were performed in aqueous medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of CDs with SEB aptamer
ce:section.ce:para: 5′ phosphorylated S1 (10 nmol) was activated by addition of EDC (10 μl, 0.2 M) and aqueous solution of 1-methyl imidazole (10 μl, 0.4 M) at pH 6 and thoroughly mixed for 90 min at 37 °C with constant stirring. To this activated 5′- phosphorimidazolide S1, 10 μl of amine functionalized CDs (1 mg/ml)was added in presence of (4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid) HEPES buffer (0.2 M, pH8) in total reaction volume of 100 μl and kept at 55 °C for 18 h.CD-S1 conjugate was purified by dialysis (MWCO-10KDa) to remove excess of coupling agents and unreacted CD and S1. The covalent conjugation of CDs with S1 was visualized by 1.5% agarose gel electrophoresis in 1X tris acetate (TAE) buffer stained with SyBr® gold and images were taken by a Canon D60 digital camera. FurtherCD-S1 covalent conjugate was annealed with partially complementary S2 strand(10 nmol) via heating up to 90 °C and then slowly cooled to 20 °C with a ramp of 0.1 °C/s and then stored at 4 °C for further downstream characterization.

For SEB detection, 1 μl (0.1 nmol) of CD-S1-S2 hybridized system and 10 μlfrom30 μM stock solution of Acfwas added to buffer solution (20 mM Tris−HCl, 20 mM MgCl2, 40 mM KCl, and 100 mM NaCl, pH 8.0) in a total volume of 90 μL and incubated for 5 min at room temperature to form the Acf intercalated CD-S1-S2 complex. This constitute the final probe solution.

The SEB standard solution stock was prepared in 0.01 M phosphate buffer saline. SEB solution of different concentrations (0.1–50 ng/mL) was added to the probe solutions separately to a total reaction volume of 100 μL, mixed well and incubated for another 15 min at room temperature. The specificity and selectivity of the developed method was tested using SEA (40 ng/mL), BSA (100 ng/ml) and supernatant of S. aureus broth (50 μL). Fluorescence intensity of the probe solution (control) and test solutions (with toxin) was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Consistency of the method in practical applications was measured by determining the recovery of fluorescence of CD in actual food and milk samples. Samples were spiked with different concentrations of SEB (0.1–50 ng/mL) and fluorescence recovery was monitored for the probe solution. Fluorescence recovery from CD in samples containing toxin was determined in test tube format (100 μL), 96 well plate format (100 μL) and PDMS microfluidic chip format (20 μL) using the exact composition of CD-S1-S2 and Acf. The recovery was demonstrated by acquiring fluorescence spectra, as well as through visual inspection over UV transilluminator or hand held UV lamp for which digital images were acquired with a Canon D60 camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of PDMS based microfluidic kit
ce:section.ce:para: The PDMS microfluidic kit was prepared following the procedure of Camargoet. al with slight modification [39]. Nylon threads having approximate diameter 200 μm were used for creating channels in the scaffold by manual wrapping of the thread on an aluminum mold. This created individual inlets, one straight and braided passive micro mixing flow channel, and another parallel and straight flow channel. The wrapping was completed when the mixer inlet reached approximately 10 mm in length. The length of the wrapped mixer was 40 mm. Next, monomers of PDMS and cure agent were poured into the mold in 10.0:1.0 w/w ratios. The mixture was degassed using ultrasonicator. After the curing, the PDMS was incubated at 95 °C for 40 min. Nylon threads were mechanically withdrawn from the polymer network using pliers. Finally, the device was hard baked at 120 °C for 20 min to complete the polymer cross-linking reactions.

The fluorescence recovery graph was ned by plotting [F/F 0- 1] in the Y-axis where F0 and Fis fluorescence intensity of control and test samples and SEB toxin concentration (ng/mL) in the X-axis. Limit of detection (LOD) was obtained by following equation [40].
(1) LOD = 3σ/m
Where, m = slope and σ = Standard error

ce:section.ce:section-title: Covalent conjugation of CD with aptamer
ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous soluble CD were synthesized by low temperature pyrolysis using citric acid and BPEI as precursors [41]. BPEI was used as a capping ligand that adorn –NH2 groups on the surface of CD. The citric acid and BPEI derived CD was characterized by FTIR, HRTEM and powder XRD (Fig. S1, SI). The average size of CD was found to be ∼5 nm in diameter as revealed from HRTEM images. The peak at ∼28° in powder XRD along with lattice spacing from HRTEM data point towards weak graphitic crystallinity. Presence of Amine functionality on CD surface was confirmed from presence of signature bands including the broad band at ∼3440 cm−1 in FTIR. The pendant amine functionality on the surface of the CD was used to covalently conjugate the CD with 5′-phosphorylated aptamer sequence S1 through EDC-NHS mediated coupling. CD-S1 covalent conjugate was subsequently purified by dialysis to eliminate excess of EDC, NHS, unreacted CD and S1 (if at all present). Aptamer sequence S1 was adapted from research reported by Degrasse [42].

The gel electrophoretic mobility of CD-S1 was lower as compared to free S1 or CD alone (Fig. 1 ) that confirms the covalent conjugation. Also, the absence of any band corresponding to S1 in the CD-S1 conjugate endorses complete conjugation of S1 with CD under the given reaction conditions. Due to the presence of numerous –NH2 groups on the surface of a CD, multiple attachment of S1 with CD is highly probable. However, the CD-S1 conjugate having a variable number of DNA strands attached to CD move as a single broad band in agarose gel. While the mobility of CD-S1-S2 (not shown in gel image) is similar to CD-S1, it further slows down after incubation with SEB with the emergence of a band corresponding to S2 that strongly points towards binding interaction between CD-S1 and SEB and subsequent release of S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic investigation of interaction of CD-DNA system with Acf and SEB
ce:section.ce:para: The signature positive peak at 281 nm and negative peak at 244 nm for Watson and Crick B-form DNA was observed for S1-S2 as well as CD-S1-S2 with circular dichroism polarimetry (Fig. 2 A). This confirms that conjugation of S1 with CD does not have any implication in duplex conformation. Slight decrease in positive and negative band-intensity was observed in the presence of Acf in CD-S1-S2 hybrid system indicative of intercalative binding of Acf molecules to DNA [43]. However, significant decreases in ellipticity for both negative and positive bands was observed in presence of SEB toxin. This is due to obvious changes in secondary structure of S1 by unwinding and conformational reorganization of its stem region to bind to the target SEB [44]. The loss of ellipticity in the circular dichroism spectra is somewhat proportional to the amount of toxin added (Fig. S2, SI).

The UV–vis absorption spectra of CD-S1-S2 show one shoulder at ∼260 nm due to the presence DNA along with an absorption peak at 350 nm corresponding to CD (Fig. 2B). Following hybridization, CD-S1-S2 was incubated with a calculated amount of Acf, so as to ensure complete intercalation of Acf in the DNA duplex of CD-S1-S2. The absorption maxima of Acf while intercalated in S1-S2 at 450 nm makes it a suitable acceptor in the FRET pair that involves the CD of CD-S1-S2 as the donor. The steady state spectra were recorded at 350 nm excitation and time-resolved fluorescence decay was recorded for CD-S1-S2 conjugates with and without Acf with a 375 nm pulsed diode laser (5 MHz) as the excitation source. The emission peak of CD at 430 nm was found unchanged in CD-S1-S2 also (Fig. 2C). Addition of Acf to the system suppress the emission of CD that point towards FRET between CD and Acf. The emission of the CD is restored upon addition of SEB. The decrease in lifetime of CDs by ∼2 ns in presence of Acf intercalated in CD-S1-S2 system indicates dynamic quenching of CD by Acf (Fig. 2D). Interestingly, addition of SEB to the aforementioned solution leads to increase in lifetime almost comparable to CD-S1-S2 that indicate disruption in the FRET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of working condition of biosensing nanoassembly
ce:section.ce:para: The binding of SEB with CD-S1-S2 was found to depend on relevant experimental factors, including Acf concentration, temperature, pH of the solution, and incubation time. Acf concentration was optimized to ensure absence of free Acf in solution and complete intercalation within S1-S2 to maximize FRET efficiency.S1 and S2 duplex formation lead to base pairing of 25 bases only whereby S1 has another 19 bases overhang (Scheme 1 , DNA sequence). Reportedly, four nucleobases in DNA bind to ∼0.25 molecules of Acf [45].Thus, for a working probe solution of 100 μL, CD-S1-S2 having approximately 0.1 nmol of S1-S2 ∼0.3 nmol of Acf was used for its intercalation with the DNA duplex. Subsequently, SEB was added to this solution and fluorescence response was recorded in the test tube format. For other formats, similar ratio of CD-S1-S2 and Acf was also maintained with reduced volume as deemed appropriate.

Incubation of the toxin with CD-S1-S2 solution leads to recovery of fluorescence where maximum response was achieved within 15 min of incubation (Fig. S3, SI). This held true for aqueous solution of SEB as well as food samples spiked up with it. The fluorescence recovery of CD-S1-S2 was found to work best in between pH 7 and 8, which essentially point out towards its suitability to work under physiological conditions (Fig. 3 A). We also evaluated the effect of temperature on the efficiency of fluorescence recovery of CD-S1-S2 after addition of SEB. Remarkably, the efficiency of fluorescence recovery remains intact from 10 to 60 °C (Fig. 3B). This result is significant that fundamentally points towards robust mechanism of operation and easy storage and usability of the biosensing system in a wide range of temperature and weather conditions. The melting temperature of S1-S2 in free form is 66 °C that increased insignificantly to 67 °C after conjugation of S1 with CD in CD-S1-S2. (Fig. S4, SI). It is to be noted that solution of CD-S1-S2 incubated with Acf does not display FRET phenomenon beyond 60 °C when irradiated with 350 nm wavelength, due to the release of Acf in solution owing to denaturation of DNA duplex. Thus, false positive is possible beyond 60 °C, which is although an improbable working condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance and sensitivity of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: After optimization of CD-S1-S2, AcF concentration, pH, temperature, incubation time, the ready to react solution was applied for the detection of SEB in various formats. In simple test tube format, a series of different concentrations of SEB toxin were added and the fluorescence recovery of CD was recorded spectrophotometrically. A saturation for the fluorescence recovery beyond 40 ng/ml of SEB toxinwas observed (Fig. 4 A). However, linear range of CD fluorescence recovery was obtained upto 10 ng/mL of SEB (Fig. 4B). The linear equation is fitted as Y = 0.075 + 0.334X with a linear correlation coefficient of R 2 = 0.9849. By using Eq. (1), the LOD obtained was 0.5 ng/mL (S/N = 3). LOD of the method developed here is comparable to that of other newly developed methods, conventional immuno-based sensing systems and instrument-based methods [46,47].

The application of this CD–aptamer based SEB detection scheme was evaluated by testing spiked samples of lentil soup and milk [48,49]. The food samples spiked with toxin showed unhindered fluorescence recovery of CD, very much similar to the aqueous solution of the toxin, signifying the viability of this biosensor system for toxin detection in food samples (Fig. S5 and S6, SI). The biosensing system displayed hardly any affinity towards SEA analog and detection of SEB was possible in the presence of the former (Fig. S7A, SI). A mixture of SEB and SEA shows fluorescence recovery similar to spiked lentil and milk samples that signifies that SEB detection is possible with the CD-Acf FRET pair in the presence of other toxins also. S. aureus broth also demonstrated fluorescence recovery since it has SEB in addition to other non-specific toxins that point towards the high selectivity of the system and its immunity against several toxin mixtures. No fluorescence recovery of CD-Acf system was observed for BSA that shows that the SEB aptamers in the CD-Acf system does not interact with substrates (random proteins) other than SEB (Fig. S7B, SI).

The SEB detection principle is based on the FRET inhibition triggered by the competitive binding of target toxin with S1 of CD-S1-S2 that results in the de-hybridization of S1-S2 leading to subsequent releases of Acf in the solution. This disruption of FRET ensures recovery of fluorescence of CD of CD-S1-S2 that could be detected by spectroscopically as well as by simple visual inspection following irradiation of the solution with a hand held UV lamp. The transition of green fluorescence to blue upon addition of SEB can be easily visualized in sample with nanogram scale spiked toxin in test tubes, 96-well plate format as well as microfluidic channel format (Figs. 5 and S6, SI) on irradiation with a UV lamp (365 nm). Moreover, the developed method exhibited a visual detection limitof 5 ng/mL, which is so far better than any other reported methods for visual detection of the toxin to the best of our knowledge [21,50]. This detection technique when translated into PDMS based microfluidic kit can detect as low as 0.1 ng of SEB in a sample of 20 μL volume just by visual inspection after illumination with UV lamp. Hence, the biosensing system could potentially be developed in a point-of-care detection system.

This is the first report of visual detection of SEB toxin in nanomolar range using CD in an aptasensing platform. SEB aptamer is covalently conjugated with intensely fluorescent CD and the former hybridized with a partially complementary smaller ssDNA. The CD act as a donor in a FRET pair where Acf, intercalated between the DNA duplex act as the acceptor. In absence of the toxin, the emission corresponds to that of Acf. However, in presence of SEB, the FRET is disrupted owing to the binding of the aptamers sequence to SEB and subsequent release of Acf in solution. In an obvious consequence, fluorescence of CD gets restored signaling presence of toxin in any experimental solution. This sensing mechanism is robust and highly selective for SEB and works well in physiological pH and in a wide range of temperature. Detection of SEB toxin was successfully realized with a LOD of ∼0.5 ng/mL by fluorescence spectrum analysis and 5 ng/mL for visual detection in presence of 365 nm UV lamp. As low as 0.1 ng of SEB could initiate a visual response of change in fluorescence emission in PDMS format. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report that demonstrate biosensing of SEB toxin with CD and aptamer using fluorometry and simultaneously visual detection that does not require any machine intervention. This definitely opens up avenues for development of a robust point-of-care yet robust biosensor for the sensitive detection of SEB in food samples in sub-nanomolar range.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: SK is thankful to DBT for fellowship. The authors are thankful to IIT Patna and SAIF-IIT Bombay for infrastructure and experimental facilities. Staphylococcus culture broth was a kind gift from Prof. K. K. Sinha and Mr. Imran of NIPER Hajipur, India that is thankfully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.09.110.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Food poisoning and Staphylococcus aureus enterotoxins
Effects of Staphylococcal enterotoxin A on the rat gastrointestinal tract
Trends in detection of warfare agents detection methods for ricin, Staphylococcal enterotoxin B and T-2 toxin

Detection of Staphylococcus enterotoxin B at picogram levels using piezoelectric-excited millimeter-sized cantilever sensors
Nanoshell-enhanced Raman spectroscopy on a microplate for Staphylococcal enterotoxin B sensing
SPR-based immunosensor for determining staphylococcal enterotoxin A
Quantitative analysis of staphylococcal enterotoxins A and B in food matrices using ultra high-performance liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS/MS)
Lab-on-a-chip for carbon nanotubes based immunoassay detection of Staphylococcal Enterotoxin B (SEB)
Comparison of LFA with PCR and RPLA in detecting SEB from isolated clinical strains of Staphylococcus aureus and its application in food samples
Rapid latex agglutination test for detection of staphylococcal enterotoxins A to E that uses high-density latex particles
Label-free nanopore biosensor for rapid and highly sensitive cocaine detection in complex biological fluids
Aptamer based biosensors for detection of Staphylococcus aureus
Nano-aptasensing in mycotoxin analysis: recent updates and progress
A quantum dot–MUC1 aptamer conjugate for targeted delivery of protoporphyrin IX and specific photokilling of cancer cells through ROS generation
Highly sensitive colorimetric biosensor for Staphylococcal enterotoxin B by a label-free aptamer and gold nanoparticles
The effect of temperature on the aggregation kinetics of partially bare gold nanoparticles
Gold nanoparticles aggregation: drastic effect of cooperative functionalities in a single molecular conjugate
Creation of linear carbon dot array with improved optical properties through controlled covalent conjugation with DNA
Biosensing of solitary and clustered abasic site DNA damage lesions with copper nanoclusters and carbon dots
Fluorescence method for quickly detecting ochratoxin A in flour and beer using nitrogen doped carbon dots and silver nanoparticles
Aptamer induced assembly of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots on gold nanoparticles for sensitive detection of AFB1
Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots for selective detection of tartrazine in food samples
Aptamer based electrochemiluminescent thrombin assay using carbon dots anchored onto silver-decorated polydopaminenanospheres
Intracellular detection of ATP using an aptamer beacon covalently linked to graphene oxide resisting nonspecific probe displacement
Highly sensitive determination of dopamine by a turn-on fluorescent biosensor based on aptamer labeled carbon dots and nano-graphite
Turbulence in microfluidics: cleanroom-free, fast, solventless, and bondless fabrication and application in high throughput liquid-liquid extraction
Limit of Blank, Limit of detection and limit of quantitation
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots for chemical sensing
A single-stranded DNA aptamer that selectively binds to Staphylococcus aureus enterotoxin B
Effects of ligand planarity on the interaction of polypyridyl Ru(II) complexes with DNA
G-quadruplex aptamer targeting Protein A and its capability to detect Staphylococcus aureus demonstrated by ELONA
A novel IgY-Aptamer hybrid system for cost-effective detection of SEB and its evaluation on food and clinical samples
A review of the methods for detection of Staphylococcus aureus enterotoxins
Detection, confirmation, and quantification of Staphylococcal Enterotoxin B in food matrixes using Liquid Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry
Novel platform for the detection of Staphylococcus aureus enterotoxin B in foods
Detection of Staphylococcus enterotoxin B (SEB) using an immunochromatographic test strip

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sonam Kumari joined IIT Patna in 2016 as PhD Scholar after her Masters in Biotechnology from Bangalore University. She was Junior Research fellow from 2016 to 2017 and currently a Senior Research Fellow funded by Department of Biotechnology, Govt. of India. Her Research interest is Carbon dot based Biosensing.

Mayank Tiwari joined IIT Patna in 2014 as an undergraduate student in the department of Chemistry at IIT Patna in the Chemical Science and Technology program. He graduated in 2018 and currently pursuing research in IISC Bangalore as project assistant. His research interest is design and development of biosensing chips and microfluidic channel based sensors.

Prolay Das received his PhD from the School of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Georgia Institute of Technology, Atlanta in 2007. After his stint as a postdoctoral fellow from 2008 to 2009 in the department of Biology at Brookhaven National Laboratory, Upton, New York, he joined IIT Patna in 2010 as an Assistant Professor in the department of Chemistry. He is currently Associate Professor at IIT Patna.
